skill.name: data-visualization-tool
skill.description: Chart and visualization generation for DBX Studio. Use when a user wants to visualize data — bar charts, line graphs, pie charts, scatter plots, etc.
skill.summary: Generate charts and visualizations from database queries in DBX Studio
skill.author: Dbxstudio

security_audit.summary: All 27 static findings are false positives. The skill is a documentation file (SKILL.md) containing markdown text and SQL examples. The static analyzer incorrectly flagged markdown backticks (code formatting) as shell commands, and misinterpreted other markdown syntax as cryptographic algorithms. No actual code execution, network calls, or system access exists in this skill.

medium_findings.title: Markdown Backtick False Positive
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged markdown backticks (`bar`, `line`, etc.) as shell command execution. These are code formatting markers in markdown, not actual shell commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: These are markdown code formatting markers (backticks) used for inline code and code blocks, not shell command execution. The file contains SQL examples and JSON configs for chart generation.

low_findings.title: Misidentified Cryptographic Patterns
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged text patterns as weak cryptographic algorithms. No cryptographic code exists in this file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file contains no cryptographic code. Static analyzer likely confused markdown text patterns. This is a documentation-only skill file.

low_findings.title: Misidentified System Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged line 79 as system reconnaissance. Line 79 contains design guidance about aggregation, not reconnaissance code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 79 says 'For large result sets, aggregate before charting' - this is data visualization best practice, not system reconnaissance.

content.value_statement: Transform database query results into meaningful charts and graphs. This skill helps Claude Code users create bar charts, line graphs, pie charts, and more directly from SQL queries in DBX Studio.
actual_capabilities: Generate bar charts for category comparisons
Create line charts for time-series trends
Build pie charts for part-to-whole breakdowns
Create scatter plots for correlation analysis
Build area charts for cumulative trends
limitations: Only works within DBX Studio environment
Requires valid SQL query for data retrieval
Cannot create custom chart types beyond listed options
Pie charts limited to 7 slices maximum

use_cases.title: Analyze Sales Trends
use_cases.description: Create line charts showing revenue over time from order data

use_cases.title: Compare Category Performance
use_cases.description: Build bar charts comparing metrics across product categories

use_cases.title: Show Data Distribution
use_cases.description: Generate histograms to understand data spread and outliers

prompt_templates.title: Basic Bar Chart
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a bar chart showing [metric] by [category] from the [table] table
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to compare values across categories

prompt_templates.title: Time Series Line Chart
prompt_templates.prompt: Show the trend of [metric] over time using a line chart for the last [time_period]
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to visualize changes over time

prompt_templates.title: Proportion Pie Chart
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a pie chart showing the breakdown of [column] as percentages
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to see part-to-whole relationships

prompt_templates.title: Correlation Scatter Plot
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a scatter plot comparing [x_metric] versus [y_metric] to see the relationship
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to identify correlations between two metrics

output_examples.input: Create a bar chart showing total revenue by product category
output_examples.output: A bar chart titled 'Revenue by Product Category' with category names on X-axis and total revenue values on Y-axis

output_examples.input: Show monthly active users trend for the past year
output_examples.output: A line chart titled 'Monthly Active Users - Past 12 Months' with months on X-axis and user count on Y-axis
A pie chart titled 'Order Status Distribution' showing percentage of orders in each status category

best_practices: Always use descriptive chart titles that include the time period when relevant
Keep axis labels human-readable rather than raw column names
Aggregate data before charting to avoid displaying raw row-level data
anti_patterns: Using pie charts with more than 7 slices makes data hard to read
Using bar charts for time-series data when line charts are clearer
Not setting meaningful titles leads to confusing chart outputs

faq.question: What chart type should I use for showing trends over time?
faq.answer: Use a line chart for trends over time. The skill automatically suggests this when you mention words like 'trend' or 'over time'.

faq.question: Can I create a chart from any SQL query?
faq.answer: Yes, as long as your query returns data with the columns needed for the x-axis and y-axis configuration.

faq.question: Why are pie charts limited to 7 slices?
faq.answer: Pie charts become hard to read with more than 7 slices. The skill recommends grouping remainder as 'Other' for larger datasets.

faq.question: How do I choose between bar and pie charts?
faq.answer: Use bar charts for comparisons and pie charts for part-to-whole relationships. The skill provides a guide for common scenarios.

faq.question: Can I customize chart colors and styling?
faq.answer: The skill focuses on data selection and chart type. Color and styling are handled by the DBX Studio visualization component.

faq.question: What if my query returns too many rows?
faq.answer: Aggregate your data in the SQL query before charting. Use GROUP BY and LIMIT to reduce the result set to a manageable size.
